channel.title: Morning Edition
channel.description: A daily curated magazine from Hacker News and Pinboard Popular.

item.title: Morning Edition — April 16, 2026
item.description:
From Hacker News

  1. Google Broke Its Promise to Me — Now ICE Has My Data
    The EFF details how Google reversed its commitment to protect user data, handing location history to immigration enforcement despite explicit opt-out settings. This is the privacy story of the week — if you use any Google services, this is a concrete case study in why "trust us" doesn't scale. The implications for anyone storing sensitive data in cloud services are immediate and chilling.
  2. The Local LLM Ecosystem Doesn't Need Ollama ⚡
    A provocative argument that Ollama has become an unnecessary abstraction layer for running local models. The author walks through llama.cpp, vLLM, and other backends that give you more control with less overhead. If you're running local models on your Mac, this is worth reading before your next setup — there may be a faster, leaner path than the one you're on.
  3. Cybersecurity Looks Like Proof of Work Now
    Drew Breunig argues that AI-powered attacks have made cybersecurity an arms race where defense now costs more than offense. Every endpoint is a surface, every model a potential tool for the attacker. The conclusion is bleak but actionable: treat security like infrastructure, not insurance. If you ship software, this reframes how you should think about every dependency you pull in.
  4. Darkbloom — Private Inference on Idle Macs ⚡
    Darkbloom turns your idle Mac into a private inference node — run LLM workloads locally with no data leaving your machine. It's the intersection of three things you care about: AI tooling, macOS, and keeping your data private. Think of it as a self-hosted alternative to cloud inference that actually respects the silicon sitting on your desk.
  5. ChatGPT for Excel
    OpenAI launched a native spreadsheet interface for ChatGPT, blurring the line between conversational AI and structured data tools. The HN comments are split between "this is the future of business software" and "this is a trojan horse for lock-in." Either way, it signals that AI companies are coming for the productivity suite, not just the chat window.
  6. Cal.com Is Going Closed Source ⚡
    Another open-source company pulls up the drawbridge. Cal.com's blog post explains the economics, but the HN thread is where the real debate lives: is the open-core model fundamentally broken, or did Cal just fail to execute? This matters for anyone who depends on OSS tools that could make the same pivot overnight.
  7. Do You Even Need a Database?
    A refreshingly honest examination of when a database is overkill. For small projects, side hustles, and prototypes, flat files, SQLite, or even localStorage might be all you need. The 268-comment HN thread is a masterclass in "it depends" — worth browsing for the real-world architecture stories alone.
  8. I Made a Terminal Pager ⚡
    A developer's love letter to the terminal, built into a modern pager that aims to replace less. It supports syntax highlighting, search, and mouse scrolling out of the box. If you live in the terminal — and you do — this is the kind of small, sharp tool that quietly improves your daily workflow.
  9. Libretto — Making AI Browser Automations Deterministic
    Saffron Health open-sourced Libretto, a framework for building browser automations that are reproducible rather than probabilistic. Instead of hoping the LLM clicks the right button, Libretto records and replays structured interaction plans. For anyone building AI-powered testing or scraping, this solves the "it worked on my machine" problem at the agent level.
  10. Agent — Native macOS Coding IDE
    A new native Mac coding environment built around agentic AI workflows — think Cursor but designed as a proper macOS citizen from day one. It uses Apple's native frameworks instead of Electron, which means it's fast, uses less memory, and integrates with the OS the way Mac apps should. Early but worth watching.
  11. Automate Work with Claude Code Routines
    The official Claude Code documentation for Routines hit Pinboard Popular — which means the broader dev community is paying attention. Routines let you schedule recurring Claude Code tasks with a prompt file, exactly like this magazine is built. If you're already using Claude Code daily, routines are the next step toward genuine automation of your repetitive workflows.
  12. They See Your Photos
    A visual demonstration of what AI can extract from the photos you upload — metadata, faces, locations, relationships, patterns. It's a visceral reminder that "sharing a photo" in 2026 means handing over a structured dataset about your life. Pair this with the Google/ICE story above and the picture gets uncomfortable fast.
  13. Google, Microsoft, Meta All Tracking You Even When You Opt Out
    An independent audit found that the biggest tech platforms ignore Global Privacy Control signals and set tracking cookies regardless of user preferences, violating California law. The companies keep paying fines because it's cheaper than compliance. If you've been relying on browser privacy controls, this is evidence that enforcement — not settings — is the only thing that works.
  14. Pretty Fish — A Better Mermaid Diagram Editor
    Mermaid diagrams are everywhere in docs and READMEs, but the editing experience has always been rough. Pretty Fish gives you a proper visual editor with live preview, drag-and-drop, and export. If you write technical documentation or architecture diagrams, this replaces the "edit text, reload, squint" loop with something that actually feels like a design tool.
  15. Steve Klabnik's Jujutsu (jj) Tutorial
    Steve Klabnik wrote a comprehensive tutorial for jj, the Git-compatible version control system that's gaining traction among developers tired of Git's footguns. Jujutsu offers anonymous branches, automatic rebasing, and undo for everything. If you've been curious about jj but didn't know where to start, this is the tutorial — from one of the best technical writers in the ecosystem.
  16. DaVinci Resolve Photo — Blackmagic's Free Photo Editor ⚡
    Blackmagic added a full photo editing module to DaVinci Resolve, bringing their color science and grading tools to stills for free. The same company that gives filmmakers a professional video editor for $0 is now doing the same for photographers. If you do any photo work and don't want to pay Adobe, this just became the most interesting free option available.
  17. Apple Frames 4 — Screenshot Framing Tool with CLI
    MacStories released Apple Frames 4, a revamped Shortcut that wraps your screenshots in realistic Apple device frames — now with a CLI for developers. You can automate screenshot framing in your build pipeline or documentation workflow. If you publish anything that includes Mac or iPhone screenshots, this is the polish tool you didn't know you needed.
  18. The Git Commands I Run Before Reading Any Code ⚡
    A practical walkthrough of the git commands that help you understand a codebase before reading a single line of source. Think git log --oneline --graph, git shortlog for contributor patterns, and git diff --stat for recent activity. Simple, immediately useful, and the kind of workflow that separates "I cloned it" from "I understand the shape of this project."
  19. Bryan Cantrill: The Peril of Laziness Lost
    Bryan Cantrill — the DTrace creator and Oxide Computer co-founder — argues that AI coding tools are eroding the productive laziness that drove good engineering. The "laziness" that made programmers automate, abstract, and simplify is being replaced by "just let the AI write it." It's a sharp, experience-backed counterpoint to the "10x productivity" narrative that everyone building with AI should sit with.
  20. The Human Cost of 10x AI Productivity ⚡
    AI shifts the bottleneck onto senior engineers whose job is validating what looks correct but may be structurally wrong. Output rises at machine speed while review, trust calibration, and accountability stay stuck at human speed. The result: worse decisions, worse sleep, more burnout, and a dangerous illusion of productivity. A necessary read for anyone leading a team that's going all-in on AI tooling.

Read the full magazine edition →

item.title: Morning Edition — April 15, 2026
item.description:
From Hacker News

  1. Claude Code Routines ⚡
    Anthropic shipped official documentation for Claude Code routines — reusable, composable workflows you can embed directly into your dev environment. This is the scaffolding for turning AI pair-programming from ad-hoc prompting into repeatable automation. If you use Claude Code daily, this changes how you structure your work.
  2. DaVinci Resolve Launches a Full Photo Editor
    Blackmagic Design expands Resolve beyond video with a dedicated photo editing module. RAW processing, color grading with the same tools filmmakers use, and it's free. The most upvoted story on HN today — the creative community is paying attention. A serious Lightroom alternative from a company that doesn't do subscriptions.
  3. Someone Bought 30 WordPress Plugins and Planted Backdoors in All of Them
    A threat actor purchased 30 popular WordPress plugins through legitimate marketplace transactions, then pushed updates containing backdoors to every user who had them installed. A supply-chain attack hiding in plain sight. If you manage any WordPress sites or advise clients who do, audit your plugin list today.
  4. jj — the CLI for Jujutsu Version Control
    $ jj init — Jujutsu is the Git replacement that treats every working copy state as a commit. No staging area, automatic rebasing, first-class conflict resolution. This tutorial by Steve Klabnik is the on-ramp the project needed. 459 comments says the dev community is seriously evaluating the switch.
  5. Stop Flock: The Fight Against Neighborhood Surveillance
    Two stories dominating HN today: a campaign to stop Flock Safety's license-plate surveillance cameras from spreading through neighborhoods, and one person's account of writing to Flock's privacy contact to opt out of their domestic monitoring program. Together they paint a picture of growing grassroots resistance to automated surveillance infrastructure being deployed without meaningful consent.
  6. Turn Your Best AI Prompts into One-Click Tools in Chrome ⚡
    Google is baking "Skills" directly into Chrome — save your most-used AI prompts as reusable one-click tools accessible from the browser toolbar. This bridges the gap between power-user prompt engineering and everyday browser workflows. If you're already building AI-assisted workflows, this is a new distribution channel for your best prompts.
  7. Google Gemma 4 Runs Natively on iPhone with Full Offline AI ⚡
    Google's Gemma 4 model now runs fully offline on iPhone hardware — no cloud, no API calls, no data leaving the device. This is the inflection point for on-device AI: capable enough to be useful, private enough to be trustworthy. Opens up entirely new categories of iOS apps that can reason locally.
  8. The Dangers of California's Legislation to Censor 3D Printing
    The EFF sounds the alarm on proposed California legislation that would require 3D printers to refuse certain print jobs — essentially building censorship into hardware. The bill's scope is broad enough to affect hobbyists, makers, and small manufacturers. A collision between safety regulation and the right to general-purpose computing.
  9. My AI-Assisted Development Workflow ⚡
    A developer shares their complete AI-assisted workflow — how they integrate LLMs into their daily coding practice, what works, what doesn't, and where the productivity gains actually land. Practical, opinionated, and full of specific tool choices. The kind of "show your setup" post that reveals real patterns.
  10. OpenDuck: Distributed DuckDB Instance
    DuckDB is already the Swiss Army knife of local analytics. OpenDuck scales it horizontally — run distributed queries across multiple DuckDB instances as if they were one database. For anyone processing datasets that outgrew a single machine but don't warrant a full data warehouse, this fills an important gap.

Pinboard Picks

  1. Superpowers — a Skills Framework for Claude Code ⚡
    An open-source skills framework that layers reusable, composable capabilities on top of Claude Code — TDD workflows, brainstorming protocols, debugging skills, and more. You're already running it. Worth tracking upstream changes and new community skills as they land.
  2. Apple Frames 4 — Device Mockups via Shortcuts, Now with a CLI
    Federico Viticci's legendary Shortcut for wrapping screenshots in pixel-perfect Apple device frames gets a major overhaul: frame color options, proportional scaling, and — the big one — an Apple Frames CLI for developers. Automate device mockups from the terminal or CI pipeline. If you ship iOS or Mac apps, this just saved you hours in Figma.
  3. Claude Cowork 2.0 — Pair Programming Evolved ⚡
    A deep look at the next iteration of Claude's cowork mode — how the collaborative coding experience has evolved, what patterns power users have discovered, and where it fits in a multi-agent workflow. If you run Claude Code sessions daily, this maps the frontier of what's possible with the latest capabilities.
  4. Native Instant Space Switching on macOS
    Kill the macOS space-switching animation entirely and get instant workspace transitions — using native APIs, no hacks. If you juggle multiple desktops (and who doesn't with a tiling window manager), this one tweak removes the single most annoying friction point in the macOS workspace flow. Tiny change, outsized quality-of-life gain.
  5. Obsidian Skills — Kepano's Plugin for AI-Powered Note Workflows ⚡
    The creator of Obsidian ships a new plugin that brings "skills" — reusable AI-powered actions — directly into the note-taking workflow. Summarize, extract, transform, and generate content within your vault using composable prompts. If Obsidian is your second brain, this gives it agency.
  6. Apfel — Free AI Running Natively on Your Mac ⚡
    A free macOS-native AI app that runs local models on Apple Silicon with zero cloud dependency. No account, no API key, no data leaving your machine. The intersection of on-device AI and Mac-first design — exactly the kind of privacy-respecting tool that's hard to find through mainstream channels.
  7. gh-stack — First-Class Stacked PRs for GitHub
    GitHub ships an official CLI extension for stacked pull requests — break large changes into reviewable, dependent PR chains that stay in sync automatically. This has been the most-requested workflow gap for teams doing trunk-based development. No more rebasing nightmare when the base PR changes.
  8. Wiretext — Design Wireframes with Unicode Characters
    A design tool that lets you build wireframes entirely from Unicode box-drawing characters — copy-paste the result into any text document, terminal, or code comment. Delightfully constrained: when your wireframe is pure text, it lives everywhere your code does. Perfect for READMEs, architecture docs, and quick prototyping without leaving the editor.
  9. They See Your Photos — What AI Extracts from Your Images
    Upload a photo and see exactly what modern AI vision models extract: location estimates, emotional state, relationships, wealth indicators, brand affiliations, and more. A visceral demonstration of how much metadata-free images still reveal to machine learning. The kind of thing that makes you rethink what you post.
  10. boringBar — A Taskbar Dock Replacement for macOS
    Replaces the macOS Dock with a Windows-style taskbar that shows open windows, workspace indicators, and system stats in a compact strip. For power users who find the Dock more decorative than functional, this reclaims vertical screen space and puts active-window management front and center. One of those "why didn't Apple do this" utilities.

Read the full magazine edition →
